( Student ’ s   signature )                              30.05.2023                                    ( Date ) ABSTRACT   Master Student's Name  Kliarovskaia  Nadezhda Academic   Advisor’s  Name  Candidate of Economics ,  Ilina   Yulia   B orisovna Master  Thesis   Title   RETURNS ON INVESTMENTS IN EQUITY CROWDFUNDING: EMPIRICAL ANALYSIS Description of the goal, tasks and main results   the research The problem of identifying the characteristics of companies attracting equity investments on crowdfunding platforms that attract investors is an important concern both in Russia and abroad. the main goal is to establish the relationship between the characteristics of companies raising equity investments on crowdfunding platforms and their attractiveness to investors. To achieve these research goals, the following tasks have been outlined and addressed: Present a literature review that explains the concepts of crowdfunding and  crowdinvesting , different types of crowdfunding and their differences, and how crowdfunding platforms work Present a literature review that analyzes the characteristics of companies involved in  crowdinvesting  and the characteristics of investors on crowdfunding platforms; Conduct an empirical study to discover the relationship between the characteristics of companies attracting equity investments on crowdfunding platforms and their attractiveness to investors; Analyze the research results, draw conclusions, and offer practical recommendations based on these findings. Research shows that older, larger firms are more attractive to investors due to perceived lower risk and stability. Patents enhance a company's appeal, highlighting perceived innovation. Investors showed preference for non-voting shares on crowdfunding platforms,  primarily due to the spread of ownership when voting shares are issued on crowdfunding platforms, which could lead to potential agency issues and increased agency costs . A larger equity stake offering correlates positively with successful fundraising, as it  signals a company's value and its commitment to shared success with its investors.  The 'Trade' industry was less preferred  with investors , while other industries showed no significant influence on investment decisions. These findings have broad practical implications for stakeholders like entrepreneurs, investors, and policy makers. Entrepreneurs can use these insights for  effective crowdfunding strategies. Investors can optimize their evaluation processes, and policy makers can frame supportive regulations, understanding the elements that contribute to crowdfunding success. Основная цель данного исследования заключается в определении взаимосвязи между свойствами таких компаний и их привлекательностью  для  инвесторов. Для достижения  указанных  целей  исследования были сформулированы и решены следующие задачи: Представить обзор литературы, посвященной понятию краудфандинга и  краудинвестинга , разнообразию типов краудфандинга и их отличий, а также особенностей работы краудфандинговых платформ. Представить обзор исследований по свойствам компаний, участвующих в  краудинвестинге , и характеристик инвесторов на краудфандинговых площадках. Провести эмпирическое исследование, целью которого является выявление связи между характеристиками компаний, привлекающих долевые инвестиции на краудфандинговых  платформах, и их привлекательностью для инвесторов. Провести анализ полученных результатов исследования с последующим формулированием выводов и практических рекомендаций. В результате исследования было выявлено, что  более зрелые и более  крупные  компании  обладают большим доверием со стороны инвесторов  и являются более привлекательными на краудфандинговых платформах , благодаря восприятию низкого риска и стабильности. Наличие патентов у компании служит дополнительным фактором  их  привлекательности  для инвесторов на краудфандинговых платформах ,  поскольку патенты  являются показателем инновационной активности и научно-исследовательской деятельности компаний . В то же время, инвесторы склонны отдавать предпочтение акциям без права голоса, чтобы избежать потенциальных агентских проблем. Предложение большей доли капитала коррелирует с успешным финансированием, сигнализируя о высоком потенциале и ценности компании.  Компании, занимающиеся торговлей,  оказалась менее привлекательн ыми  для инвесторов, тогда как другие отрасли не демонстрировали значительного влияния на решения о вложениях. Такие выводы имеют большое практическое значение для различных стейкхолдеров, включая предпринимателей, инвесторов и политиков. Предприниматели могут использовать эти знания для создания эффективных стратегий краудфандинга. Инвесторы могут оптимизировать процессы оценки, а регулирующие органы могут учитывать эти факторы при формулировании поддерживающих мер, направленных на способствование успеху краудфандинговых инициатив. Introduction The digital revolution has significantly disrupted traditional financial models, paving the way for innovative methods of capital raising, such as crowdfunding. Crowdfunding, facilitated by internet services and digital platforms, democratizes access to capital, enabling entrepreneurs to appeal directly to a wide pool of potential investors [Colombo et al., 2020]. In recent years, global crowdinvesting volumes have shown rapid growth.  I n 2020, the global market size was assessed to be around USD 5.48 billion. The sector is predicted to continue expanding at an estimated compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 8.3% from 2021 to 2028 [Market Insights, 2022]. This growth trajectory has been influenced by an array of factors such as regulatory evolution, cultural variables, and dynamic market conditions [Ziegler et al., 2019].   ​ In Russia, the Central Bank analyzed the crowdfunding market in 2020, revealing high growth rates. In 2020, the total funds raised amounted to 7 billion rubles, while in 2021, the figure nearly doubled to 13.8 billion rubles [Bank of Russia, 2022]. In  crowdinvesting  in 2021, issuers managed to attract 4.74 billion rubles through this method, and in the first quarter of 2022, they raised 1.06 billion rubles [Bank of Russia, 2022].   Various countries have taken unique regulatory approaches to crowdfunding. In the US, the Jumpstart Our Business Startups (JOBS) Act was enacted in 2012 to facilitate equity crowdfunding. Likewise, in the UK, the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) provides a comprehensive regulatory framework for crowdfunding [Brown et al., 2019].   In Russia, legislation governing crowdfunding platforms was only passed recently in 2020, suggesting the sector's nascent emergence and potential for growth in the Russian financial landscape. Despite the significant growth and interest in crowdfunding, a gap remains in academic literature regarding the factors that contribute to a successful equity crowdfunding campaign. Previous studies have explored some dynamics of crowdfunding, but there remains limited understanding of the impact of company age, size, patent registration, and the nature of shares offered on equity crowdfunding success [Hornuf and Schwienbacher, 2020; Walthoff-Borm et al., 2019]. Given the rapid evolution of the global crowdfunding landscape, updated research that reflects these changes and delivers valuable insights to investors, entrepreneurs, and regulators is paramount. This study aims to address this research gap by exploring the determinants of success in equity crowdfunding, using data from the UK, a mature crowdfunding market, and extrapolating the implications for emerging markets, including Russia. The motivation behind this research is to equip stakeholders with practical insights, thereby fostering the sustainable growth of the crowdfunding sector. The  problem  of identifying the characteristics of  companies attracting equity investments  on crowdfunding platforms that  attract investors  is an important concern both in Russia and abroad. The  research question  raised in this study is: "Which characteristics of companies  raising  equity investments on crowdfunding platforms  attract investors ?" As a result, the main  goal  is to establish the relationship between the characteristics of companies  raising  equity investments on crowdfunding platforms and th eir attractiveness to investors . To achieve these research goals, the following  tasks  have been outlined and addressed: Present a literature review that explains the concepts of crowdfunding and  crowdinvesting ,  different types of crowdfunding and their differences, and  how crowdfunding platforms work Present a literature review that   analyzes  the characteristics of companies involved in  crowdinvesting  and the characteristics of investors on crowdfunding platforms ; Conduct an empirical study to discover the relationship between the characteristics of  companies attracting equity investments  on crowdfunding platforms and their  attractiveness to investors ; Analyze  the research results, draw conclusions, and offer practical recommendations based on these findings. The research  object  comprises companies attracting funds on crowdfunding platforms. The  subject  of the study is the relationship between the characteristics of  companies attracting equity investments  on crowdfunding platforms and the financial performance of such companies. This study has been conducted in the form of an  empirical investigation , employing statistical and econometric analysis tools. The study's sample comprised of 741  crowdfunding campaigns   that have raised equity investments on the English crowdfunding platform, Crowdcube, from 2014 to 2023. The data for this empirical study was obtained from the following sources: Crowdcube crowdfunding platform; Amadeos database; The official website of the UK Companies House. This study proposes several hypotheses related to what makes  companies attracting equity investments  appealing to potential investors: H1: Companies attracting equity investments on crowdfunding platforms with registered patents, are more attractive to potential investors compared to companies without registered patents . H2: Companies attracting equity investments on crowdfunding platforms offering investors non-voting shares are more attractive to potential investors than companies offering voting shares. H3: There is an inverse relationship between the equity stake offered by the company raising equity investments on the crowdfunding platform to potential investors and its attractiveness to potential investors. H4.1: Manufacturing companies raising investments on crowdfunding platforms are more attractive to potential investors than companies from other sectors. H4.2: IT companies raising investments on crowdfunding platforms are more attractive to potential investors than companies from other sectors. H4.3: Trade companies raising investments on crowdfunding platforms are more attractive to potential investors than companies from other sectors. H4.4: Tech companies raising investments on crowdfunding platforms are more attractive to potential investors than companies from other sectors. The structure of this study includes an introduction, three chapters, a conclusion, a list of references, and appendices. The first chapter provides an overview of the theoretical foundations of crowd investing. It introduces the concept of crowdfunding, explores the history of its emergence, the types of crowdfunding, their distinguishing features, and the operating principle of crowdfunding platforms. It also introduces the concept of crowd investing. The second chapter presents an analysis of investor and company characteristics that draw funds on crowdfunding platforms. It also explores the motivation of investors who finance companies on crowdfunding platforms, as well as the motivations of companies attracting equity investments on crowdfunding platforms. In the third chapter, research hypotheses are formulated to determine the correlation between the characteristics of  companies attracting equity investments  on crowdfunding platforms and their appeal to investors. This appeal is reflected in the number of investors investing money in companies on crowdfunding platforms, and the "success" indicator, which is calculated as the ratio of the sum of funds raised on the crowdfunding platform to the initially declared sum of money that the company aims to raise on the crowdfunding platform. The chapter presents the research methodology, describes the data collection process, and the sample formation. An analysis of descriptive statistics and the results of the econometric study are provided. Conclusions and practical recommendations are given based on the results of the conducted research. These findings  of this study have  broad practical implications for stakeholders like entrepreneurs, investors, and policy makers. Entrepreneurs can use these insights for effective crowdfunding strategies. Investors can optimize their evaluation processes, and policy makers can frame supportive regulations, understanding the elements that contribute to crowdfunding success. 2.  Theoretical background 2 .1. The  concept of crowdfunding Crowdfunding, an innovative concept that emerged in recent years, has dramatically shifted the landscape of entrepreneurship and investment. It has been referred to as a "paradigm shift in entrepreneurship"  [ Short et al., 2019 ] , as it has democratised access to capital, enabling a wide array of individuals and businesses to realize their creative and entrepreneurial projects. Furthermore, the  [ World Economic Forum ,  2021 ]  has acknowledged the role of crowdfunding as a tool for social innovation. This practice of raising funds online from a large number of people leverages the power of the crowd, removing geographical barriers and allowing a diverse range of ideas to be funded  [ Brown et al., 2020 ] . As  authors  pointed out, this form of financing allows firms to raise capital without the need for traditional financial intermediaries, leading to a disruption in the financial services sector. Crowdfunding’s history can be traced back to the early 2000s with the advent of donation-based platforms like ArtistShare. However, it was the establishment of Kickstarter in 2009 that truly marked the beginning of the rise of crowdfunding. The success stories of projects receiving considerable funds from the crowd sparked public interest, leading to the launch of similar platforms worldwide  [ Agrawal et al., 2020 ] . The passing of the JOBS Act in 2012 in the U.S was a significant regulatory turning point, marking the formal recognition of crowdfunding as a legitimate method of raising capital  [ Belleflamme et al., 2019 ] . The legislation not only enabled equity crowdfunding but also signaled the disruptive potential of crowdfunding to the conventional financial system. Crowdfunding's growth trajectory continued through the next decade, with the development of new models such as peer-to-peer lending, equity crowdfunding, and even initial coin offerings (ICOs). As stated by  [ Davies ,  202 2 ] , crowdfunding has now evolved from being a peripheral funding method to a mainstream alternative to traditional financing. As a relatively new phenomenon, crowdfunding has been defined in various ways across the academic literature.  [ Belleflamme et al., 2014 ]  view crowdfunding as an open call over the internet for financial resources in the form of a monetary donation.  [ Schwienbacher and Larralde ,  2012 ]  perceive crowdfunding as the financing of a project by a group of individuals instead of professional parties, thereby emphasizing the collective effort. On the other hand,  [ Mollick ,  2014 ]  stresses the internet-enabled aspect of crowdfunding, underscoring the importance of the digital platform in bridging geographically dispersed individuals with entrepreneurs. More recent definitions, such as those by  [ Ziegler et al., 2019 ] , highlight the heterogeneity of crowdfunding types, distinguishing between donation, reward, lending, and equity models.  [ Ahlers et al., 2015 ]   note that crowdfunding allows entrepreneurs to circumvent traditional financing sources while obtaining market validation for their products or services. Similarly,  [ Estrin et al., 2018 ]  emphasize the role of crowdfunding in closing the funding gap for early-stage ventures. While these definitions may differ, they all echo the same underlying themes: the use of internet-based platforms, collective effort, and bypassing traditional financial intermediaries. Several key stakeholders are involved in the crowdfunding ecosystem: The project creators or companies seeking funds range from start-ups and SMEs to established businesses looking to innovate or expand their operations  [ Dorfleitner et al., 20 22 ] . Their motivations to opt for crowdfunding can be diverse, from the need for seed capital, product validation to marketing exposure  [ Moritz et al., 2020 ] .   Entrepreneurs who use crowdfunding platforms to finance a company are often those who fail to raise capital through traditional channels [Bruton et al., 20 15 ]. Crowdfunding allows not only to raise capital for a business but also to evaluate the attractiveness of a business idea. If a large group of people is ready to invest money in a company, this means that many people are interested in receiving a product or service, and the idea is attractive [Belleflamme et al., 2019]. Furthermore, crowdfunding campaigns can help entrepreneurs gain valuable feedback, market insights, and potentially develop a loyal customer base [Anglin et al., 2021] . The investors or backers provide the capital. They can be individuals or institutional backers and are often motivated by factors such as potential returns, supporting innovation, or philanthropic reasons. It's interesting to note that the relationship between backers and creators extends beyond the financial aspect, often forming a community around the project or venture  [ Parhankangas, 20 19 ] .   Crowdfunding democratizes the access to capital for startups, allowing regular people (meaning non-professional investors) to participate in the decision-making process of which ideas are worth turning into a business [Davies & Giovannetti, 2020]. Moreover, investors have the opportunity to receive something in return, for example, financial gain, public coverage of the investor's name, securities, etc. The availability and type of reward depend on the types of crowdfunding, which will be discussed later [ Lin and Viswanathan, 2016 ]. Crowdfunding platforms act as intermediaries, providing the necessary infrastructure and services to facilitate the process. They play a pivotal role in managing and maintaining trust between the project creators and backers  [ Hornuf  et al., 20 17 ] .   These websites have the classic sharing economy business model, which is to attract investors and entrepreneurs to the platform, where the former can choose the company they want to invest in, and the latter can attract investments in the company. Crowdfunding platforms play a crucial role in the process by providing necessary information, reducing transaction costs, and mitigating potential risks for investors. Thus, a full definition of crowdfunding should emphasize not only the most important features of the method but also the relationship between three significant stakeholders. In this paper, crowdfunding will be defined as a method of raising capital from a large group of investors who invest certain amounts of money through Internet intermediaries, and crowdfunding platforms. 2 .2. Types of crowdfunding In crowdfunding, there are several models that serve different purposes depending on the desired outcome.  T hese models can be classified into four main types: donation-based, rewards-based, debt-based, and equity-based crowdfunding. Each model follows a unique set of principles and caters to different types of projects, industries, and investment needs. In the following sections, we'll take a closer look at each type and provide examples of successful campaigns within them. Donation-based Crowdfunding Donation-based crowdfunding involves soliciting financial contributions from a group of individuals who donate their funds towards a common cause, expecting no monetary return. Often employed for social, community or personal causes, this model leverages the goodwill and charitable instincts of the crowd  [ Ziegler et al., 2020 ] . Importantly, this type of crowdfunding may also attract philanthropic organizations and individuals seeking to support causes that resonate with their interests.   Donation-based crowdfunding is predominantly used by non-profit organizations to attract donations for specific projects. Unlike traditional charity, donations are collected and earmarked for a specific project, which can increase donors' willingness to contribute larger sums. The main areas of fundraising include charitable purposes (e.g., helping seriously ill people, orphans, and the disabled), social projects (e.g., landscaping gardens and parks, filming, performing in theaters), and political activities (e.g., election campaigns) [Davies  et al. , 20 18 ]. Charitable investors are more loyal in the long run when non-profits keep them updated on progress, and the main motivation for these investors is social impact. Researchers have also found that donation-based crowdfunding can increase the efficiency and transparency of charitable giving, making it more appealing to donors. A significant example of this model was seen in the wake of the devastating fire that engulfed the Notre-Dame Cathedral in Paris in 2019. Multiple crowdfunding platforms launched campaigns to support the cathedral's restoration, with campaigns on GoFundMe and JustGiving alone raising millions of Euros. The success of these campaigns demonstrated how donation-based crowdfunding can marshal support for significant cultural and heritage causes on a global scale  [ Baeck et al., 2020 ] . Another example is the Omaze platform, which combines fundraising for charity with the chance to win unique experiences. From meeting celebrities to exclusive trips, the platform has raised millions of dollars for various charitable causes, demonstrating the potential of donation-based crowdfunding when paired with innovative reward systems. Rewards-based Crowdfunding Rewards-based crowdfunding, as the name suggests, offers backers a non-monetary reward related to the project or venture in return for their contribution. This could be a product, a service, or a unique experience related to the project. This model has been particularly effective for creative projects like films, music albums, art installations, and product innovations.   This type of crowdfunding is popular among creative projects, such as films, music albums, video games, and new gadgets, as well as for startups and small businesses looking to launch innovative products [Mollick & Nanda, 201 6 ]. Backers typically receive tiered rewards depending on their level of contribution. These rewards can range from simple thank-you messages to exclusive access to the finished product or unique experiences with the project creators [Burtch et al., 20 18 ]. Researchers have found that rewards-based crowdfunding can help entrepreneurs test the market and build a community around their product or idea, as well as increase customer engagement and loyalty. The Pebble Time Smartwatch project on Kickstarter remains one of the most iconic examples of this model's success. The campaign raised over $20 million, far exceeding its initial target and helping to bring the innovative smartwatch to market. This model enables creators to validate market demand before full-scale production and fosters a community of early supporters around a product  [ Block et al., 2018 ] . Debt-based Crowdfunding or Crowdlending This crowdfunding model, also known as crowdlending, involves individuals or groups lending money to a project, a business, or an individual. The expectation here is a return of the original sum lent, along with interest after a set period. It offers an alternative to traditional bank loans, especially for small and medium-sized enterprises  ( SMEs ) , and is a more formal and  financially focused model compared to the previous two  [ Hornuf et al., 2021 ] .   Investors in debt-based crowdfunding platforms can diversify their investment portfolios and potentially earn higher returns compared to traditional savings accounts, albeit with higher risks [ Wallmeroth , 2019]. These platforms often use algorithms and credit-scoring models to assess  companies ' creditworthiness, helping to mitigate risks for investors [Serrano-Cinca et al., 20 15 ]. The growth of crowdlending has also been attributed to the increasing adoption of financial technologies (fintech) and the expanding sharing economy [Zhang et al., 20 19 ]. Funding Circle, a prominent peer-to-peer lending platform, offers an example of how crowdlending can facilitate loans for small businesses. By matching investors looking for attractive returns with businesses seeking loans, platforms like Funding Circle help to fill the funding gap faced by many SMEs. Similarly, the Wind farm Energia Verde Ventuno in Italy raised over €2.5 million through the European crowdlending platform October, showing the potential of debt-based crowdfunding in the renewable energy sector. Equity Crowdfunding or Crowdinvesting Equity crowdfunding allows a broad group of investors to fund start-up companies and small businesses in return for equity. Investors give money to a business and receive ownership of a small piece of that business. If the business succeeds, they make a profit; if it fails, they lose their investment.   Different authors propose various definitions for crowdinvesting. According to [ Belleflamme et al, 2014 ], crowdinvesting is a model where investors obtain a stake in a company's equity through the purchase of shares or by entering into specific agreements (such as profit-sharing agreements) via online crowdfunding platforms. [ Cumming et al, 2016 ] emphasize that the primary distinction between crowdinvesting and traditional share offerings lies in the funding process itself: entrepreneurs make an open call for funding on a crowdfunding platform, and investors make investment decisions based on the information provided there. [Alonso et al., 2020] define crowdinvesting as a financing method where a company sells shares to a group of investors through an open call for funding on internet platforms. The authors further note that the main difference between crowdinvesting and other types of crowdfunding is that investors invest in the company's equity capital and become owners of its shares. It should be noted that the type of investor remuneration may vary depending on country regulation. For example, in the UK, an investor who has invested in a company on a crowdfunding platform becomes the owner of its shares, which the company issues after raising funds on the platform. While in Germany, an investor who has invested money in a company on a crowdfunding platform agrees with the company to share in its profits and does not receive shares [ Hornuf  et al., 2018]. In this paper, equity  crowdinvesting  will be defined as a method of raising capital from a large group of investors who invest certain amounts of money in exchange for a company’s shares through Internet intermediaries, and crowdfunding platforms. One of the most prominent examples of this model's success is Brewdog, a UK-based craft beer company. Through its "Equity for Punks" campaign, Brewdog raised more than £26 million from the public. Similarly, the UK-based digital bank Monzo has raised significant capital through equity crowdfunding rounds, engaging its customers and creating a robust brand community. Companies can issue and offer various types of shares to investors on crowdfunding platforms. For example, on the UK crowdfunding platform  Crowdcube , companies have the opportunity to place type A shares, the owners of which voting rights and equal rights in capital distribution and dividends, and type B shares, which do not have voting rights. Holders of type B shares do not have the right to vote at general meetings of shareholders but have equal rights in capital distribution and dividends [ Crowdcube , 202 3 ; Cumming et al., 2018]. The data presented by  [ Million Insights ,  2022 ]  highlights the notable growth in the global  crowdinvesting  sector. In 2020, the market size was measured to be around USD 5.48 billion. Further analysis shows that this sector is expected to continue to expand, with an estimated compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 8.3% from 2021 to 2028  (Picture 1) . This information indicates that  crowdinvesting  is becoming an increasingly important part of the crowdfunding landscape and is expected to grow steadily in the near future. Global  crowdinvesting  volume, 201 9 -202 8  ( bln  USD) Source : [ Million Insights ,  2022 ] In addition to being a fast-growing technology, crowdfunding addresses the funding gap for  early-stage  businesses, allowing them to get the  initial  funding before moving on to more  sophisticated forms of funding, such as angel and venture capital investments and IPOs [ Lukkarinen  et al., 2016]. Thus, this process allows funding for startups that might not otherwise have access to capital. 2 .3.  How crowdfunding platforms work Crowdfunding platforms act as intermediaries that facilitate the process of raising funds from a large group of individuals, often for specific projects or ventures. Particularly for equity crowdfunding or crowdinvesting platforms, the workflow is typically delineated into several key stages: Registration : The first step is for the entrepreneur or business to register on the chosen platform. Registration typically requires the submission of certain documents, such as the company's legal registration details, proof of identity of the company's owners, and sometimes even a credit check or other financial information, to verify the authenticity of the business. Proposal Submission : After registration, the venture submits a comprehensive proposal detailing the business plan, market analysis, financial projections, potential returns for investors, and how the funds raised will be utilised. Due Diligence : This is a critical step in which the platform conducts a thorough investigation of the venture's viability, financial health, market size, and leadership team. The due diligence process can either be managed in-house by the platform or outsourced to an external party.  For example, on the UK crowdfunding platform  Crowdcube , financial due diligence is carried out for each company that intends to raise funds on this platform by third-party providers such as  Creditsafe , Experian, and  Onfido . [ Crowdcube , 202 3 ].   The aim is to reduce risk and ensure that the venture presents a legitimate and viable investment opportunity.   Based on the results of due diligence, for each company that is going to raise funds on the platform, its pre- money valuation  is calculated. Campaign Launch : The fundraising campaign goes live only after the proposal has passed the due diligence stage. At this point, the venture has to set a target fundraising amount that they aim to achieve. Potential investors can browse through various ventures, review proposals, and decide whether they would like to invest.   Companies upload financial statements that will be available to investors registered on the crowdfunding platform. Investment : The actual process of raising funds begins after the campaign launch. Investors registered on the platform review various ventures, examining their unique characteristics, and make their investment decisions. Ventures often use different  marketing strategies, like email newsletters, social media advertising, and press releases to attract potential investors.   Recent studies show that advertising companies increase the chances of attracting investments on a crowdfunding platform [ Belleflamme  et al., 2014;  Mollick , 2014]. During the fundraising campaign, the project owner must keep his investors informed about the development process of the project Post-investment Management : The platform's role doesn't end after the successful completion of the fundraising campaign. Depending on the platform, they may provide services like managing the share registry, providing updates to investors, or facilitating further rounds of funding. Platforms also differ in how they handle the raised funds.  The activity of the first type of crowdfunding platform is that the funds are kept in a deposit account and, if the required amount, which was originally chosen by the company as the ultimate goal, is not collected, the deposits are returned to investors. Another type of crowdfunding platform allows companies to keep all the funds raised, even if the original goal was not achieved [ Valanciene  L.,  Jegeleviciute  S., 2013]. Thus, after the end of the fundraising, either the companies receive the funds, or the funds are returned to the investors. Share Issuance : If a venture successfully reaches its fundraising goal, it then issues shares to its investors, proportionate to their respective investments. This rewards investors with an ownership stake in the company  [De  Buysere  et al., 2012]. The actual process may vary across platforms and regions due to different regulatory frameworks. However, the steps outlined above provide a general overview of how most equity crowdfunding or crowdinvesting platforms operate. Thus, in the first chapter, the concept of crowdfunding was formulated as a method for companies to raise funds from a large group of investors who invest specific sums through online intermediaries, namely, crowdfunding platforms. Subsequently, the history of crowdfunding was explored. Additionally, the following types of crowdfunding were examined: charity crowdfunding, which involves raising funds on a crowdfunding platform on a non-repayable basis, reward-based crowdfunding, where companies attract funds on crowdfunding platforms in exchange for minor non-financial rewards for investors, peer-to-peer lending, a method of voluntary collective financing of legal or natural persons through crowdfunding platforms, and crowd investing. The definition of crowd investing was then introduced as follows: "crowd investing is a method of attracting funds from a large group of investors who invest certain sums in exchange for company shares through online intermediaries - crowdfunding platforms".  We identified that  for equity crowdfunding platforms, the workflow is generally broken down into  several stages: Registration, Proposal Submission, Due Diligence, Campaign Launch, Investment, Post-investment Management and Share Issuance. 3. Characteristics of companies and investors on crowdfunding platforms 3 . 1 .  Characteristics of investors on crowdfunding platforms Investors on crowdfunding platforms come from diverse backgrounds, exhibiting varying characteristics depending on their interests, motivations, and risk appetites. As the crowdfunding landscape spans across various models such as donation-based, rewards-based, debt-based, and equity-based crowdfunding, the characteristics of investors tend to differ accordingly  [ Barasinska et al., 2019 ] . Donation-based crowdfunding : Investors in donation-based crowdfunding are typically individuals who contribute small amounts of money to support causes or projects they feel passionate about. They might be motivated by personal affinity, altruistic considerations, or social reasons. These investors do not expect a monetary return on their investment; instead, they derive satisfaction and fulfilment from contributing to a cause they believe in  [ Hornuf and Schwienbacher, 2017 ] . Rewards-based crowdfunding : Investors in rewards-based crowdfunding are often driven by a desire to gain early access to a product or service , in other words, pre-purchase motive , or by the novelty of supporting an innovative idea. They are typically consumers who value the product or service offered and are prepared to fund its development in return for being among the first to access it once it's ready  [ Belleflamme et al., 2020 ] .  These investors expect a non-monetary reward, often in the form of the product or service that the project is aiming to develop. They tend to be more risk-tolerant and are usually motivated by the prospect of supporting innovative ideas. Debt-based crowdfunding : Investors in debt-based crowdfunding or crowdlending are usually individuals or institutions looking for a stable return on their investment. They lend money to a project or business with the expectation that they will receive their investment back with interest. These investors are often risk-averse, looking for safer investments with predictable returns  [ Bruton et al., 201 5 ] .  Investment decisions of these investors are heavily influenced by the credibility and creditworthiness of the borrower. Equity-based crowdfunding .  Investors in  equity  crowdfunding expect a financial return on their investment, similar to traditional venture capital or angel investors  [ Ahlers et al., 2015 ] .  The study [ Cholakova ,  Clarysse , 2015] confirms that, despite the fact that altruistic and internal motivating factors have a positive correlation with the desire to invest money, they do not play such a big role in making an investment decision. That is, when choosing a company for investment, investors are less guided by moral considerations. The biggest  motivating factor for investors is the future positive economic value and financial returns as it is important for investors to get a return on their investment and maximize profits. Focusing on equity crowdfunding or crowdinvesting, we can identify several types of investors, each with distinct characteristics: Friends and family  of entrepreneurs. Early investors in crowdfunding are usually people with whom the founders of the company raising funds have social contacts, such as close friends and family members who have a personal interest in the success of the project [ Mollick , 2013] Retail  or non-professional  investors : These are individual investors who invest relatively small amounts of their personal funds. They might be driven by a variety of motivations, including a belief in the product or service, the desire to support entrepreneurship, and the hope of a financial return. Retail investors typically have a high risk tolerance and are prepared to lose their investments.   However, they often invest in projects that align with their interests and values, demonstrating a willingness to take on risk for the potential of high returns  [ Vulkan et al., 2016 ] .  Retail  investors often lack experience in investing, they do not have the opportunity to evaluate companies (for example, evaluate financial performance and certain characteristics of the company's founders) in the same way as professional investors do [ Freear , et al., 1994]. The study [ Ahlers  et al., 2015] confirms that non-professional investors lack the experience and capacity to evaluate various investment projects on crowdfunding platforms. In addition, compared to investments made by non-professional investors, the costs of company valuation are too high. It makes no economic sense for potential non-professional investors to spend time conducting due diligence companies, since the return on investment will be less than the cost of conducting it [Cumming, ‎Johan, 2019]. [Agrawal et al., 2013] in their study confirm that non-professional investors in  crowdinvesting  have a limited ability to conduct due diligence due to the presence of fairly fixed costs for one project. According to the Regulatory Approach to Internet Crowdfunding, non-professional investors can invest no more than 10% of their annual income on crowdfunding platforms; Accredited  or professional  investors  are individuals or business entities that are legally recognized to deal in securities and have a higher threshold for investment risk due to their net worth or income level. These investors typically have a sophisticated understanding of the market and are driven primarily by the potential for high returns  [ Hornuf & Schwienbacher, 2018 ] .   Professional investors investing a large share of their own capital on crowdfunding platforms. According to the classification of the UK Financial Conduct  Authority, professional investors who invest a large share of equity are considered to be investors with an annual income of at least £100,000 or net assets of at least £250,000 . Angel investors : Angel investors are affluent individuals who invest larger amounts, often in early-stage ventures. They typically have industry experience and offer mentorship and networking opportunities in addition to their investment  [ Walthoff-Borm et al., 2018 ] . Institutional investors : These are organizations that invest large amounts of money. They might include venture capital firms, hedge funds, pension funds, and family offices. Institutional investors are typically risk-averse and invest in ventures with a strong potential for high returns. They often participate in later-stage crowdfunding rounds, once the venture has proven its viability  [ Cumming & Hornuf, 2018 ] . Syndicate investors : These are groups of investors who pool their resources to invest together, often led by an experienced angel investor or venture capitalist. Syndicates allow individual investors to participate in larger deals that they might not be able to afford individually. This type of investor is often found on platforms that cater to more sophisticated or accredited investors  [ Signori & Vismara, 2018 ] . Other companies/strategic investors . Other companies may decide to invest in companies on crowdfunding platforms that they consider to be of strategic importance to them [ Schwienbache r , 2010]. While these general categories can give us an idea of the types of investors present on equity crowdfunding platforms, it's essential to note that the lines can be blurry, with many investors fitting into multiple categories or shifting categories over time. The heterogeneity of investors in crowdfunding is one of the factors contributing to its dynamism and complexity. Crowdfunding platforms do not disclose the identity of business angels and venture capitalists who invest on the platforms. According to a study [Wang et al., 2019], the share of professional investors on crowdfunding platforms is 1%. The UK Government has invested £1.25m in 9 projects through the London Co-Investment Fund. Thus, most investors on crowdfunding platforms are non-professional investors who invest small amounts, not exceeding 10% of the total annual income. 3 . 2 .  Characteristics of  c ompanies on  c rowdfunding  p latforms The crowdfunding landscape is populated by a diverse set of companies, spanning numerous industries and stages of growth. This diversity largely hinges on the type of  crowdfunding model in play, including donation-based, rewards-based, debt-based, or equity-based crowdfunding. Donation-based crowdfunding : Companies leveraging donation-based crowdfunding are frequently not-for-profit entities, charitable organizations, or individual proponents raising funds for a specific cause. These campaigns are fundamentally rooted in a cause or a mission rather than profit-seeking. For instance, medical campaigns for individuals, funding drives for disaster relief, and environmental initiatives are often run on this model. The key distinguishing characteristic of such companies is their purpose-driven ethos, and their success hinges on the emotive resonance of their cause with the audience.  [ Ziegler et al. ,  2019 ]  note that these campaigns are essentially about 'giving' rather than investment, and thus don't promise financial returns to the contributors. Rewards-based crowdfunding : This model predominantly attracts early-stage startups or individuals with innovative product ideas. Companies in this category are often characterized by their unique value proposition or a compelling innovation that is appealing to potential customers. The campaign usually offers the product or service as a 'reward' to contributors, serving dual purposes of fundraising and market validation. This model is widely adopted by creators in technology, design, arts, and similar fields.  [ Mollick ,  2014 ]  outlines that, for these campaigns, a tangible prototype or a clear demonstration of the concept greatly enhances the chances of success. Debt-based crowdfunding : Debt-based crowdfunding or crowdlending is an attractive option for SMEs (small and medium-sized enterprises) and individuals requiring loans. These companies typically have established operations, revenue streams, and the capability to service a debt. It's like a digital peer-to-peer lending mechanism where investors earn interest on their loaned amount.  [ Vulkan et al. ,  2016 ]  identify the reduced cost and complexity of borrowing compared to traditional channels as a primary reason for SMEs' preference for this model. When it comes to  equity crowdfunding or crowdinvesting , the characterizing attributes of participating companies are considerably more varied. Nonetheless, it's common to encounter early-stage and growth-oriented companies in this sphere, primarily owing to the nature of equity investment. These companies are usually distinguished by their high-growth potential, innovative offerings, and a need for capital to fuel expansion or development plans  [ Hornuf & Schwienbacher, 2018 ] . Regulatory environments across countries directly influence the profiles of companies raising capital via equity crowdfunding. For example, the SEC regulations in the US, instituted  under the JOBS Act, cap the amount a company can raise through equity crowdfunding platforms at $1.07 million annually  [ SEC, 202 3 ] . This regulatory constraint encourages smaller, early-stage companies to take advantage of equity crowdfunding. Contrarily, in the UK, there's no explicit limit imposed by the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) on the amount that a company can raise via equity crowdfunding. However, companies are required to adhere to strict disclosure standards and issue robust risk warnings to potential investors  [ FCA, 202 3 ] . Consequently, the UK equity crowdfunding landscape witnesses a broader range of companies, including mature and larger enterprises, raising funds via this model. Meanwhile, in Canada, the maximum amount a company can raise varies by province, ranging from CAD 250,000 to CAD 1.5 million, as per different securities regulatory bodies  [ Canadian Securities Administrators, 202 3 ] . In  Australia, there are clear criteria that a company must meet when raising investments on crowdfunding platforms. Thus, the company must have a certain legal form, its shares must not be traded on the stock exchange, the main activity must be conducted in Australia, the majority of directors must be residents of Australia, the value of the company's assets must not exceed 25 million Australian dollars, and the consolidated annual revenue companies must not exceed 25 million Australian dollars. However, in some  other countries, for example, in Russia, there are no criteria that restrict the activities of certain companies on crowdfunding platforms. The legislation of these countries specifies only the maximum amounts that companies can raise on the platforms. Across all jurisdictions, however, companies opting for equity crowdfunding share a common attribute: ambition for growth. These businesses, armed with a compelling vision and business model, aim to harness the power of the crowd to realize their growth aspirations. Equity crowdfunding platforms offer them an opportunity to bypass traditional . Crowdinvesting  allows companies that are not of interest to venture capitalists, business angels and banks to raise financing. For example, thanks to the development of  crowdinvesting , crowdfunding platforms attract investments from companies operating in the creative industries [Bao, Huang, 2017], as well as social companies [ Parhankangas  et al., H., 2019]. 3 . 3 . C haracteristics  of companies  attract ing  investors on crowdfunding platforms Equity crowdfunding continues to gain traction, but its exploration lags behind traditional funding methods [Cumming and Vismara, 2017; Vismara 2016, 2018; Walthoff-Borm et al.,  2018]. This is primarily due to the proprietary nature of private companies that utilize crowdfunding platforms, which typically withhold their financial or other internal data. The difficulty in acquiring such information, especially pertaining to investor traits on crowdfunding platforms, poses challenges to research [Giudici et al., 2020]. Despite these obstacles, crowdfunding emerges as an influential tool for entrepreneurs, promoting the evolution and formation of new theories [Cumming et al., 2019]. Crowdfunding volumes have seen a significant surge in recent years. Studies on crowdinvesting are also numerous, however, little focus has been given to the governance of companies raising funds on crowdfunding platforms [Walthoff, Borm et al., 2018]. Present research on equity crowdfunding mainly falls into two categories. The first explores the factors that contribute to a company's success in raising funds through crowdfunding. These include the features and traits of a company that investors find appealing [Ahlers et al., 2015; Block et al., 2017; Hornuf and Schwienbacher, 2015, 2017; Ralcheva and Roosenboom, 2016; Vismara, 2016, 2017; Vulkan et al., 2016]. Factors such as holding registered patents [Hornuf and Schwienbacher, 2015], equity share, offering voting shares, and the age of the company play significant roles [Ralcheva and Roosenboom, 2016]. Company size is also a major consideration [Vulkan et al., 2016]. Additional research focuses on the relationship between company characteristics and their subsequent performance [Hornuf and Schmitt, 2017; Signori and Vismara, 2016], as well as how these traits affect a company's future performance post a crowdfunding campaign [Signori and Vismara, 2017; Vismara, 2018; Walthoff-Borm et al., 2018]. The second category of studies examines why entrepreneurs opt for equity crowdfunding as a funding source [Estrin et al., 2018]. Let's delve deeper into the characteristics that tend to attract investors on crowdfunding platforms and could potentially indicate future financial performance. Non-voting shares:  At Crowdcube, a UK-based crowdfunding platform, companies have the option to offer both voting and non-voting shares [Cumming et al., 2018]. When there's a division between a company's ownership and control, it can result in a conflict of interest between the management and the shareholders, which can subsequently lead to higher costs [Jensen, Meckling, 1976]. Issuing non-voting shares can help circumvent this issue. Companies that provide non-voting shares often appeal more to investors and typically raise more capital compared to those offering voting shares [Cumming et al., 2019]. Registered patents:   Companies that  implement innovations, as a result of investment in  R&D , gain a competitive advantage and create potential for future  high financial   performance. Patents are the principal performance indicator of research and development and a measure of innovation [Chang et al., 2012] .   C ompanies with registered patents have a competitive advantage due to pursuing innovations; legal guaranties against competition in product markets and protection of the intellectual property [Hsu and  Ziedonis , 2013]. Patents may influence a firm’s future financing and survival since they allow start-up companies to protect their intellectual property and may signify that companies have their own developments that might be used in future to obtain greater profit .  Companies with registered patents are more attractive to investors on crowdfunding platforms. [Cumming et al., 2016;  Hornuf ,  Schwienbacher , 2017] . Patents can shield a start-up's intellectual property, signifying that the company has unique developments that could be leveraged for higher profits in the future. Market diversification:  Diversification across different markets is often linked with better financial performance [Christensen and Montgomery, 1981]. Diversified companies are usually more attractive to investors [Signori and Vismara, 2016]. This could be due to the reduced risk associated with diversified operations and the potential for tapping into multiple revenue streams. Equity stake:  The size of the equity stake a company offers on a crowdfunding platform also matters. Companies offering larger equity stakes could attract more investors as it provides them with a larger potential share in the company's future profits. Industry specificity:  Finally, the industry in which the company operates can significantly affect its attractiveness to investors. For instance, tech and IT companies tend to attract more investors due to their high-growth potential and the pervasiveness of technology in our daily lives [Vulkan et al., 2016]. Additionally, companies operating in environmentally friendly or socially responsible industries may appeal to investors who prioritize ethical investing [Belleflamme et al., 2014]. The industry specificity serves as an indicator of potential profitability, market size, and growth, thereby influencing investor decisions. Founders' Background and Experience:  Investors often consider the background and experience of the founders or management team of the company. Having founders or leaders with a track record of successful ventures, or industry-specific expertise, could signal a higher likelihood of success and lower risk to potential investors [Ahlers et al., 2015]. Social Capital:  Companies with high social capital, such as a large following on social media or a strong network of industry connections, may attract more investors. Social  capital can enhance trust and signal the firm's popularity, credibility, and potential customer base, which could translate into financial performance [Vismara, 2016]. Financial Forecasts:  The presence of solid financial forecasts could be an indication of the company's business acumen and its future prospects. Detailed, achievable financial projections can give investors confidence in the potential return on their investment [Ralcheva and Roosenboom, 2016]. External Validation:  Companies that have already received funding from reputable sources (such as venture capitalists, angel investors, or government grants) may be more attractive to crowdfunding investors. Such external validation can serve as a signal of the company's potential and decrease the perceived risk for investors [Ahlers et al., 2015]. Geographical Location:  The location of a company could impact its ability to attract investors. Companies located in regions known for a particular industry (like Silicon Valley for tech) or in areas with a high concentration of entrepreneurial activities, might attract more investors [Lin and Viswanathan, 2016]. This may be due to investors' perception of these regions as having a vibrant startup ecosystem, supportive policies, or a pool of talented resources. In  second c hapter, we categorize investors on equity crowdfunding platforms into seven distinct groups. The first category includes friends and family of entrepreneurs who have a personal stake in the success of the venture. Retail or non-professional investors make up a large portion of these platforms, investing small personal amounts with varying motivations but often lacking extensive experience in evaluating companies. Accredited or professional investors bring market sophistication and are largely driven by high return potentials.  Other categories include  angel investors, institutional investors, syndicate investors, and strategic investors.  T he majority of investors on these platforms are non-professional, investing in relatively small amounts, with professional investors accounting for a mere 1% as per a recent study. Companies participating in crowdfunding platforms span across diverse industries and growth stages. Their specific characteristics are often determined by the crowdfunding model they embrace: donation-based, rewards-based, debt-based, or equity-based. Equity crowdfunding, or crowdinvesting, draws a broader spectrum of companies. However, they are predominantly early-stage, innovative, growth-oriented enterprises seeking capital for expansion or development. The regulatory environments of different countries also play a role in shaping the profiles of companies  that raise capital via equity crowdfunding. Despite these variations, all companies opting for equity crowdfunding exhibit a shared trait: a desire for growth. Also  we identified s everal factors contribute to a company's success in equity crowdfunding, from attracting investors to securing substantial capital.   Key determinants of success in attracting investors in equity crowdfunding include: issuance of non-voting shares, ownership of registered patents, market diversification, offered equity stake size, industry specificity, founders' background and experience, level of social capital, financial forecast accuracy, external validation, and geographical location. These factors play a pivotal role in driving investor appeal and ensuring successful capital raising. 4 .  E mpirical study 4 .1. Research questions and hypothes e s As of today, there are several research studies investigating the characteristics of companies that are attractive to investors on crowdfunding platforms. Some of these studies are dedicated to the factors that influence the success of a crowdfunding campaign on such platforms. They explore what characteristics of companies affect whether they can attract a sufficient amount of investment (see, for example, [Ahlers et al., 2015; Hornuf and Schwienbacher, 2015, Hornuf and Schwienbacher, 2017; Ralcheva and Roosenboom, 2016; Vismara, 201 8 ; Vulkan et al., 2016]). I nnovation plays in the attractiveness of a firm to potential investors.  [ Schumpeter , 2008]  broad understanding of innovation encapsulates numerous combinations, such as new product creation, novel production methods, exploration of new markets, and acquisition of raw materials, among others. The  [ Oslo Manual ,  2010 ]  further elucidates this concept by defining innovation as the introduction of novel or significantly improved products, processes, marketing methods, or organizational methods in various areas of business. Innovation, according to these definitions, is a transformative process that takes an initial idea through a series of stages until it becomes a product, service, or production method of practical significance and potential competitive advantage  [ OECD, 2002 ] . It is through innovation that companies generate a competitive edge and additional profit, aligning with the companies' objective function  [ Safronov, 2008 ] . A key element of this innovative process is successful research and development (R&D) and experimental design. The outcomes of these activities are often embodied in patents, making patents crucial indicators of research outcomes  [ Chang, Chen, Huang, 2012 ] . Patents provide companies with an exclusive right over their inventions, creating a legal bulwark against market competition and offering a competitive advantage to patent holders  [ Hsu and Ziedonis, 2013 ] . Patents can play a significant role in a firm's financial trajectory and survival chances. They offer protection to young companies, enabling them to guard their intellectual property and signaling the presence of proprietary developments that may deliver higher returns in the future  [ De Vries, Pennings, Block, J.H., Fisch, 2017 ] . In this context, potential investors often view patents as trustworthy indicators of a company's technological value and knowledge base. Companies with larger patent portfolios are generally perceived to possess superior research capabilities and to be more invested in innovative activities. Therefore, patent activity becomes a sign of companies that have navigated through the  earliest and most challenging stages of the innovation process, demonstrating their ability to finalize R&D projects and transform them into profitable assets. The association between patents and investment attraction has been explored in numerous studies, and a similar trend is observed in the crowdfunding context.  [ Ahlers et al. ,  2015 ] , for instance, analyzed a sample of 104 companies that raised funds on an American crowdfunding platform between 2009 and 2012. They found a positive correlation between the presence of patents and the total amount raised, reinforcing the notion that patents provide credible signals of potential for future returns. They used a binary variable, set to 1 if the company raised more than the initial target and 0 if it did not, similar to the model used by  [ Hornuf and Schwienbacher ,  2017 ] . This indicates that investors place significant value on the presence of patents when deciding where to invest on crowdfunding platforms. Moreover,  [ Ralcheva and Roosenboom ,  2016 ]  investigated 74 Dutch firms seeking crowdfunding between 2012 and 2015. They revealed that firms holding patents were more likely to achieve their crowdfunding goals, providing further evidence of the influence of patents on investor decision-making. The researchers found that firms that exceeded their fundraising goals were more likely to possess registered patents, reinforcing the importance of intellectual property in attracting crowdfunding investments. [ Mollick and Nanda ,  2016 ]  also explored the impact of patents on crowdfunding success. Analyzing 2011-2013 data from 300 US-based companies on a leading crowdfunding platform, they discovered that companies with patents were more successful in reaching their funding goals. Not only did these companies raise more funds, but they also attracted a larger number of backers, reinforcing the view that patents enhance the attractiveness of companies to potential investors. Collectively, these studies provide substantial empirical support for your hypothesis, showing that patents are positively associated with attracting equity investments on crowdfunding platforms. The presence of patents is seen as a credible signal of a firm's innovative capability and potential for future success, thus boosting its appeal to potential investors. Thus, the first research hypothesis was formulated as follows: H1: Companies   attract ing  equity investments on crowdfunding platforms  with  registered patents, are more attractive to potential investors compared to  companies without registered patents . The concept of voting rights and their implications in shareholder structures is central to the study of corporate governance and finds its roots in seminal works of corporate theory. [ Berle and  Means ,  1932 ]  are often credited with first describing the agency problem in their classic work, where a corporation with ownership scattered among a large number of small shareholders results in a situation where the shareholders are incapable of influencing the  company's management. Managers thus  have  exclusive management rights, effectively creating a separation between ownership and control. This idea laid the groundwork for future exploration into the nuances of corporate governance and the role of shareholders within a company.   Building on this foundational theory,  [ Jensen and Meckling ,  1974 ]  elucidated the inherent agency problems that result from this divide, where the interests of managers and shareholders diverge due to informational asymmetry, thus opening the door for potential opportunistic behavior from managers. Jensen and Meckling's work provides a comprehensive exploration of the agency cost theory, highlighting the costs arising from this separation, which includes monitoring costs, bonding costs, and residual losses.  [ Fama and Jensen ,  1983 ]  further deepened the understanding of the agency problem by examining the role of decision-making within a firm and the potential for conflicts of interest. They argued that the separation of decision management (initiating and implementing plans) and decision control (ratifying and monitoring plans) leads to agency problems, echoing the insights from Berle and Means. The concept of the separation of ownership and control has also been a significant topic during initial public offerings (IPO). For instance,  [ Gompers et al. ,  2010 ;  Bebchuk and Kastiel ,  2017 ]  concurred that agency costs increase during IPOs due to this separation. They offered crucial insights into how the agency problem unfolds in IPOs and the resultant impact on agency costs. The issue of shares with different voting rights is seen as a mechanism to establish corporate control and reduce agency costs, a concept extensively explored by  [ Grossman and Hart ,  1988 ] . They proposed that the best allocation of control rights is one that maximizes the total benefits from a company's activities and highlighted the role of voting rights in managing agency problems. Their work underscores the importance of voting rights in shaping the firm's performance, governance, and investor relations. In light of these theories, the nature of shares issued by  companies attracting equity investments  on crowdfunding platforms becomes a vital factor for potential investors. For instance, UK companies can issue both voting and non-voting shares on crowdfunding platforms, according to  [ Crowdcube ,  202 3] . Shareholders of Type B shares may not have voting rights, but they enjoy equal rights to dividends and cash flows. Consequently, this leads to a division of cash flow rights and control rights. Conversely, issuing Type A shares triggers a separation of ownership from control, thereby increasing agency costs  [ Cumming et al., 2016 ] . This theoretical background finds support in several empirical studies.  [ Cumming et al. ,  2016 ] , for instance, analyzed 491 companies raising funds on Crowdcube between 2011 and 2015. They found that the companies issuing Type A (voting) shares were less successful in raising funds on the platform, attracting fewer investors. This inverse relationship signifies that the separation  of ownership from control diminishes the likelihood of companies attracting the necessary funds on the crowdfunding platform. [ Vismara ,  2016 ]  provides additional evidence, showing that companies offering only non-voting shares were more successful in crowdfunding campaigns. After studying 145 firms that raised equity investment on an Italian crowdfunding platform between 2014 and 2016, he found that companies offering non-voting shares were more likely to reach their funding goals. Similar conclusions were drawn by  [ Estrin et al. ,  2018 ] , who analyzed 87 firms seeking funding on a French crowdfunding platform. They observed that firms offering voting shares attracted fewer investors and were less likely to achieve their funding targets. Together, these studies give credence to the  statement  that companies offering non-voting shares on crowdfunding platforms are more attractive to potential investors, owing to the reduced agency costs and absence of the ownership-control divide.   Thus, the second research hypothesis was formulated as follows: H2:  C ompanies attracting equity investments  on crowdfunding platforms offering investors non-voting shares are more attractive to potential investors than companies offering voting shares. The size of the equity stake offered by a  companies attracting equity investments   on a crowdfunding platform has significant implications for both the company and potential investors. It directly reflects how much control and future cash flow potential investors can expect to receive. This concept aligns with several classic works in corporate governance and financial theory. [ Modigliani and Miller ,  1958 ]  seminal paper on the irrelevance of capital structure in a frictionless market laid the groundwork for subsequent studies. According to their theorem, a firm's value is determined by its real assets, not by its capital structure. However, this theory was built on a set of ideal assumptions, including no taxes, no transaction costs, and no information asymmetry, which rarely hold in reality. Later,  [ Jensen and Meckling ,  1976 ]  proposed the agency theory, which suggested that when a firm's ownership is dispersed among many shareholders, an agency problem arises due to the separation of ownership and control. As the equity stake offered by companies on crowdfunding platforms increases, this separation is amplified, leading to increased agency costs. These increased costs can act as a deterrent to potential investors, making the  compan y  attracting equity investments  less attractive. In the same vein,  [ Myers and Majluf ,  1984 ]  introduced the pecking order theory, suggesting that companies prefer internal financing over external financing to avoid issuing new equity because of information asymmetry between company insiders and potential investors. This theory  implies that companies offering larger equity stakes might be perceived as riskier by potential investors, which can make them less attractive. Empirically, several studies have investigated the relationship between the equity stake offered by companies on crowdfunding platforms and their attractiveness to potential investors. For instance,  [ Vismara ,  201 8 ]  analyzed a sample of 111 companies that attracted equity investments in 2014 on a British crowdfunding platform. The findings suggested an inverse relationship between the equity stake offered by the company and its attractiveness to investors. [ Ahlers et al. ,  2015 ]  echoed this sentiment in their study of 104  companies attracting equity investments  on a crowdfunding platform between 2006-2011. They found an inverse relationship between the equity stake offered and the number of investors investing in the company on the crowdfunding platform.  Another noteworthy study was conducted by [Vulkan,  Åstebro , and Sierra, 2016]. They found that the equity stake offered negatively impacts the probability of the campaign reaching its target. The more equity the company offered, the less likely it was to reach its fundraising goal, suggesting that potential investors might view a larger equity stake as a signal of desperation or high risk. However, some researchers offer contrasting findings.  [ Ralcheva and Roosenboom ,  2016 ]  examined a sample of 541  companies attracting equity investments   on the Crowdcube crowdfunding platform between 2012-2015. They found a direct relationship between the equity stake offered to potential investors on crowdfunding platforms and the ratio of raised funds to the initial goal. They suggested that companies offering a larger equity stake might attract more funds for large-scale projects, thereby enticing potential investors. Finally,  [ Hornuf and Schwienbacher ,  2017 ]  carried out a detailed analysis of equity crowdfunding in Germany and the UK. They discovered that a larger equity stake didn't necessarily attract more investors. Instead, the study concluded that investors often preferred investing in campaigns that offered less equity, suggesting an inverse relationship. Given these varying viewpoints, it's essential to further investigate this relationship, leading to the formulation of our third research hypothesis: H3: There is an inverse relationship between the equity stake offered by the  compan y   raising  equity investments  on the crowdfunding platform to potential investors and its attractiveness to potential investors. The industry sector of a company plays a fundamental role in shaping its attractiveness to investors on crowdfunding platforms. As each industry comes with its unique landscape of risks and rewards, potential investors may base their decisions on the industry familiarity, anticipated growth, perceived innovation, and a variety of other sector-specific factors. Tech companies represent a sector that has often been viewed as an epicenter of innovation, constantly pushing boundaries and bringing forth cutting-edge technologies. This sector encompasses a diverse range of companies, from those developing sophisticated artificial intelligence systems to those creating user-friendly mobile applications.  an exemplary instance is MindMaze, a Swiss neurotechnology company that raised an impressive £1.3 million in 2016 on a crowdfunding platform, exceeding its target by 369%  [ Crowdcube , 2023] . MindMaze specializes in creating virtual reality interfaces for the rehabilitation of stroke and neurological disorder patients. Its innovative healthcare technology attracted 121 investors, resulting in a highly successful crowdfunding campaign, which demonstrated the appeal of ground-breaking tech solutions in the eyes of crowdfunding investors . The tech industry's innate proclivity for disruption, typically brought about by their innovative products or services, can offer high growth potential. Their scalability and global market appeal frequently contribute to significant investment returns. Research by  [ Vulkan, Åstebro, and Sierra ,  2016 ]  that involved a sample of 446 crowdfunding campaigns, for example, reported a notably higher success rate in the tech sector. Their regression analysis showed that tech-oriented companies were 35% more likely to meet their funding targets than companies in other sectors. This might suggest that investors perceive tech companies as an avenue for potentially higher returns, thereby rendering them more attractive. Manufacturing companies, on the other hand, are often seen as pillars of stability within the investment landscape. These companies, ranging from makers of durable goods like machinery to producers of consumer goods like clothing, typically operate within well-established markets with predictable demand patterns.  In the realm of manufacturing, Miso Robotics, an American company that designs adaptive robots for commercial kitchens, offers an outstanding example. Its flagship product, Flippy, an autonomous robotic kitchen assistant, attracted substantial investor interest during the crowdfunding campaign on Crowdcube  [ Crowdcube , 2023] . With a target of £1 million, the company raised £2.29 million, exceeding its initial goal by 229%, with the participation of 820 investors. This showcases the attractiveness of advanced manufacturing solutions to investors, who appreciate the potential of robotic technology and its capability to revolutionize industries. Unlike the volatile nature of tech firms, manufacturing companies' steady revenue generation, combined with their tangible assets, can be appealing for investors seeking stable returns. In an empirical study involving 104 equity crowdfunding campaigns,  [ Ahlers et al. ,  2015 ]  found that companies in sectors with tangible assets, such as manufacturing, attracted more substantial investments. Their results demonstrated a positive coefficient for manufacturing companies, suggesting a direct relationship with the invested amount. Trade companies, typically encompassing retail businesses, wholesale traders, and import-export firms, offer another set of unique investment considerations.  Sugru, a UK-based trade company specializing in consumer goods, provides an impressive example of a successful crowdfunding campaign. The company produces and sells an innovative mouldable glue that turns into rubber, a product with a wide range of applications. Sugru's crowdfunding campaign successfully raised £3.39 million from 2327 investors, surpassing its goal by 337%  [ Crowdcube , 2023] . The product's uniqueness and broad applicability, coupled with a strong business plan, garnered significant attention from potential investors .  [ Belleflamme, Lambert, and Schwienbacher ,  2014 ]  analysis of 588 crowdfunding campaigns found trade and retail companies to be successful, thanks to their ability to showcase market validation. Finally, the IT sector houses companies that provide a wide range of products and services, from software development to IT consulting. An example of success in this area is  eMoov, an online real estate agent from the UK. eMoov offers an innovative and cost-effective service for selling homes online, disrupting the traditional real estate agency industry. The company's crowdfunding campaign attracted a multitude of investors, with 795 people investing in eMoov, leading to a successful fundraising round that secured £2.6 million, 260% over the initial goal  [ Crowdcube , 2023] . eMoov is a testament to the attractiveness of IT companies that provide innovative solutions, have the potential for high market penetration, and represent a viable alternative to traditional industry incumbents . The innovative nature of IT companies, coupled with the potential for high market penetration and growth, can be highly attractive for investors.  [ Hornuf and Schwienbacher ,  2017 ]  study of 1,272 crowdfunding campaigns revealed that IT projects had a higher likelihood of achieving their funding goals than projects from other sectors. Thus, the  hypotheses  were  formulate d as  following: H4.1: Manufacturing companies raising investments on crowdfunding platforms are more attractive to potential investors than companies from other sectors. H4.2: IT companies raising investments on crowdfunding platforms are more attractive to potential investors than companies from other sectors. H4.3: Trade companies raising investments on crowdfunding platforms are more attractive to potential investors than companies from other sectors. H4.4: Tech companies raising investments on crowdfunding platforms are more attractive to potential investors than companies from other sectors. 4 . 2 .
